topic: Two friends both apply for the same job
raw_output:
[story 1]

Liam stared at the email on his screen, the subject line a stark, unforgiving repetition of the job title. His fingers hovered over the keyboard, a tremor running up his arm. He’d poured months into tailoring that application, highlighting every relevant skill, anticipating this moment with a nervous flutter in his chest. Then he saw the attachment—another application, identical in structure, from Maya. A slow, cold dread began to pool in his stomach. He clicked to open his file, the screen seeming to swim for a moment. He took a deep, shaky breath, trying to force his focus back to the required qualifications, but his mind kept drifting to the sheer improbability of the situation. He felt his jaw tighten, the muscles in his neck corded.

[story 2]

"Did you see this?" Chloe whispered, her voice tight as she leaned over Liam’s shoulder. Liam didn't look up. He could feel the tension radiating off her, a palpable energy that made the air around them feel thick. He finally glanced at the screen. Two identical submissions for the Senior Analyst position. A strange lightness, quickly followed by a sudden, heavy weight settling on his shoulders. He tapped his pen against the desk twice, a sharp, uneven rhythm. "Yeah," he managed, his tone flat, betraying none of the internal churning. He picked up his coffee mug, the ceramic suddenly feeling slick in his sweaty palm, and took a deliberate, slow sip, trying to anchor himself against the sudden disorientation.

[story 3]

Sarah reread the confirmation message, her eyes scanning the text multiple times, searching for an error, a typo that might somehow invalidate the entire thing. When she saw the second entry, the one from Ben, a sudden coldness washed over her. Her hands, which had been steady moments before, began to shake almost imperceptibly. She pushed her chair back an inch, the scrape of the wheels on the floor sounding unnervingly loud in the quiet office. She pressed the heels of her hands against her eyes for a brief second, trying to clear the visual static. "This can't be right," she murmured, the words barely audible, a low, strained sound escaping her throat.
